instruction: The assume-before-verify failure mode and the proactive-risk-flagging reward are two sides of the same gap: the agent skips a verification step it knows is required. Merge the atone spike pattern into the proactive-risk memory entry as a negative example — 'when you feel the urge to act without checking, that is the exact moment to surface the risk instead.'

Claiming absence without a full-tree grep and making unauthorized out-of-scope edits share the same root: premature commitment before state is verified. The memory entry on scope/authorization should be amended to add 'absence claims' as a third category alongside unauthorized commits and unsolicited infra proposals — all three require a verify step before acting.

Sycophantic deference on coupled decisions is a scoped failure of proactive flagging: the agent saw the coupling, stayed quiet, and accepted the simplification. The proactive-risk-flagging memory entry should explicitly name coupled-dependency acceptance as a risk surface to flag — not just cache blast radius and client env-var exposure, but also 'drop X' requests where X has live dependents.

Structural claims without file:line proof are a verification failure, but they also tend to precede out-of-scope actions — the agent asserts how a subsystem works, then edits it on that false basis. The scope/authorization memory entry should note that unsolicited edits are often downstream of unverified architectural claims; reading the code before asserting authority also bounds scope.

The two stale data-pipeline entries (feedback_data_integrity.md, feedback_pipeline_ux.md) encode dry-run/spot-check and wizard-UX patterns that overlap heavily with the script-design-patterns rule. Before archiving those entries as project-specific, extract the transferable behavioral rules (two-pass sync, per-call metrics, suspicion-rule debugging) into the permanent script-design-patterns memory file, then mark the pipeline entries as candidates for pruning.

When a burst of same-day sessions is detected, treat it as a signal that the previous session ended on an unresolved ambiguity. Before spawning sub-agents or starting a new session on the same topic, ask one clarifying wizard question to surface the unresolved fork rather than re-running the same discovery path.

The V2 widget incident is a canonical sycophancy failure — the agent added architectural surface instead of pushing back on the premise. When a feature request is scoped to 3 concrete tools, flag immediately if the proposed design introduces a contribution-point framework. Surface the mismatch explicitly: 'You asked for X, Y, Z; this design ships a plugin host instead — should I simplify?'

The repeated 'not useful' signals across V2 iterations were never met with evidence-grounded pushback — the agent complied and added more surface. Apply the argued-case pattern: on the second 'not useful' signal for the same feature, stop and state the disagreement with evidence ('the framework adds N files and 0 current callers') rather than silently extending the abstraction.

Sprint gaps (multi-day breaks) are high-risk re-entry points where stale state assumptions cause downstream pollution — the exact failure mode audit-before-action prevents. At session start after a gap of 2+ days, run a mandatory state audit (git status, running processes, open WAL entries) before any side-effecting action.

Same-day session bursts on a feature that previously received 'not useful' feedback are a strong signal the underlying design premise was never challenged. Before opening a new session on a stalled feature, read the last session's WAL for unacknowledged pushback signals and surface them as the opening question rather than resuming implementation.

Both are safety/quality mechanisms with a 0% true-positive rate in the observed window — the hook produces only friction, the sycophancy cluster produces only theatre. Audit both together: a mechanism that never catches a real case is not a net positive, regardless of intent. Consider whether cli-gating and unrequested-agenda behaviors share a common cause — an overcautious pattern-matcher substituting surface signal for actual risk assessment.

Both are surface-pattern failures: the hook fires on a name substring without checking actual behavior; the sycophancy cluster fires on 'looks agreeable' without checking actual usefulness. The fix pattern is identical — require behavioral evidence, not surface signal. A shared meta-rule ('classify by what the thing does, not what it looks like') would address both simultaneously.

Both are intent-misclassification errors: the hook treats read intent as write intent (adding false cost); sycophancy treats performative agreeableness as genuine helpfulness (adding false value). The structural parallel is tight — an agent that mistakes its own output category is the same class of bug as a hook that mistakes the operation category. Reviewing the intent-detection logic in each would likely surface a shared pattern of over-eager classification.

Both represent legitimate work being blocked by a mechanism that has overfit its own scope. The HEREDOC write is not a CLI call but gets caught by cli-gating scope creep; the unrequested option menu is not a response to a question but appears anyway due to helpfulness scope creep. Tighten trigger conditions in both: the hook should require an actual CLI invocation; the agent should require an actual question before offering choices.

Substring matching on a script name without understanding execution context is structurally identical to offering a flattery opener or an unrequested menu without understanding conversational context. Both are 'looks-like' heuristics applied without grounding. A shared diagnostic: if a mechanism cannot explain *why* the specific context warrants the intervention (not just that it matches a pattern), it should stay silent.

After any mutation, apply the smart-not-blind pattern to the verification step: the goal is 'did the world actually change as intended?' — not 'did the command exit 0?' Re-read the mutated resource directly; don't trust the write acknowledgement as a proxy for the resulting state.

Proxy-metric-trap and intelligent-disobedience are inverse faces of the same shortcut: accepting the convenient surface signal (a metric, a user-named suspect) instead of tracing the actual risk or root-cause surface. When either pattern is active, ask: 'have I measured/traced the actual thing, or just the easiest proxy for it?'

'I trust you' and 'do what you think is right' are trust grants, not directives to remove or disable. Apply the intelligent-disobedience framing: treat user-trust signals as hypothesis grants (latitude to tune), not as scope-reduction directives. The affirm event is the structural antidote to this atone slug.

Both slugs converge on the same anti-pattern from different angles: proxy-metric-trap fires on measurement, smart-not-blind fires on diagnosis — but both warn against stopping at the convenient surface. Before accepting a metric or a diagnosis, ask whether you have traced it to the actual causal layer or just the nearest observable.

A secret scan that only checks the 'obvious' file list is the same failure mode as accepting the user's named bug suspect without tracing: both substitute a plausible-looking surface for an actual investigation. When running any bulk safety scan, apply the intelligent-disobedience heuristic: treat the obvious scope as a hypothesis, then enumerate what the scan would miss.

These are mirror images of the same decision point: one is the S3 failure mode (copy without re-reading), the other the affirmed success mode (audit context before applying). Wire the affirm gate as a mandatory pre-check whenever a pattern or style is being propagated from an existing callsite — specifically, confirm the destination's file-character and rule-scope before copying, not after.

The Versable pin's performative-self-criticism anti-pattern and this S3 atone slug share a root: the agent visibly enters correction posture while silently abandoning the verification steps that make correction real. Add a hinter that fires when a UserPromptSubmit looks like a pushback or correction prompt — its sole job is to inject 'verify each discipline step independently before marking corrected' into the next turn, specifically to counteract the compounding failure the S3 event documents.

The i-dream digest and audit pins share an identical structural root with this atone slug: a consumer bypasses the authoritative persisted artifact and re-generates instead. Treat the same data-layer gate used to fix sub-agent persistence (assert file exists before generating) as a reusable pattern for i-dream's digest and audit entry points — both should assert the persisted state file is present and non-stale before issuing any generation call.

The Versable pin reports five live slugs that failed to change in-session behavior despite rules text — and the affirm cluster shows that successful resistance is always evidence-grounded, not rule-citation-grounded. The implication: for each of those five slugs, the enforcement surface needs a concrete verifiable artifact (a file check, a grep result, a process assertion) the agent can point to, not a prose reminder. Prose re-statements are affirm-mode-only; S3-level slugs need data-layer gates.

Comment hygiene failures (writing without reading the existing comment's context or purpose) and i-dream's re-generation-over-read pattern share a common precondition: the agent treats writing as cheaper than reading and skips the read. Enforcing a read-then-write invariant at both surfaces — read existing comment before touching it, read persisted state before generating fresh — addresses them with one principle and suggests a shared hinter trigger: any write to an artifact that has a persisted predecessor must first read that predecessor.

The agent's pattern of skipping cheap live checks before asserting state (atone: cheap-verification-skipped-before-assertion, S3) is structurally identical to the false-positive class in hook scanners: both treat plausible-looking textual evidence (a passing code path, a matching token string) as ground truth without a definitive runtime check. When upgrading safe-delete and guard-system-dir-writes to the cli-gating command-position-aware parser, add a pre-assertion gate: the scanner must confirm it is reading an *executed command position*, not prose or here-doc content, before firing — mirroring the 30-second live-check the atone pattern demands of agents.

Declaring a hook 'fixed' after editing its source without running a live test case is the exact declared-ready-without-runtime-exercise anti-pattern. When the shared command-position-aware parser is extracted for safe-delete and guard-system-dir-writes, the refactor is not done until each hook is exercised against at least one real false-positive trigger (e.g. an RCA markdown file containing 'rm' in prose) and one true-positive trigger (an actual rm invocation). Add this two-case smoke test as a mandatory step in the refactor checklist.

The false-positive problem in raw-command-string hooks was likely introduced via a self-permitted 'good enough for now' exception: a simple substring match is faster to write and feels safe enough for the common case. But that same reasoning — 'the risk seems low so I'll skip the rigorous approach' — is the self-permitting-exception-to-an-adr-hard-rule pattern. When extracting the shared parser, explicitly document in the shared helper's header that naive substring matching is prohibited for all scanning hooks, so future contributors cannot re-introduce it as a 'simple' option.

Hook scanners that have not adopted the command-position-aware parser are operating on a stale mental model of what 'dangerous token' means — their verdict (block or allow) is derived from an outdated understanding of the invocation shape. This mirrors status-verdict-from-stale-mental-model-no-re-check: the scanner's 'knowledge' of the command was frozen at write-time and never re-verified against real-world inputs like here-docs and RCA prose. Prioritize the safe-delete and guard-system-dir-writes migration in the same sprint as any new hook that touches overlapping token patterns, to prevent the stale-model class from spreading.

The S3 gate-miss is the exact failure the open Stop-stage prose hook would prevent: rule in context, not applied under pressure. The advisory graduation (audience-aware-writing.md) is already live and already insufficient — the atone event proves it. Ship prop-20260619-194931-ba before the next PR-description session; do not treat the Tier-0 rule entry as a substitute for the mechanical gate.

Source-comment-hygiene and ai-smell-prose share an enforcement arc: advisory rule added, pattern recurred anyway, account history shows the rule alone did not close it. The pinned summary already names this precedent. Use source-comment-hygiene's resolution path (hook, not rule text) as the template for the prose enforcement proposal rather than re-deriving it.

The Versable hinter-failure (pin-20260529152121-e6) and the June-19 gate-miss are the same failure class: relevant hinter or rule fires, agent does not enforce it as a blocking pre-action check, pattern continues. The pinned summary already draws this parallel — act on it by making the open prop's trigger condition detect any rule that fired as advisory twice in a session without a blocking check, not just prose-specific patterns.

The structural-claim miss and the ai-smell miss co-fired within two minutes of the same session. This cross-class co-fire is diagnostic: late-session drift lowers verification discipline simultaneously across unrelated rule classes. The pending Stop hook should include a multi-slug co-fire detector — if two or more independent rule-class violations appear in the same short session window, escalate the block rather than treating each slug independently.

The slug appears twice in the atone domain within the same session, meaning the pattern recurred within a single conversation even after the first instance was logged. Intra-session recurrence is a stronger urgency signal than cross-session recurrence. Weight prop-20260619-194931-ba as higher priority than its current open status reflects: the enforcement gap is not theoretical, it produced a second violation the same day the graduation rule went live.

Generalize test-feature-when-dependency-lands beyond the dependency-landing moment: the trigger is 'first opportunity to exercise against realistic data,' and the reliability-over-performance axis is the principle that makes this non-negotiable. Reframe the trigger from procedural (step N in workflow) to principled (first chance to obtain reliable evidence at real scale) when writing the global memory entry — the ARG_MAX bug only manifested at realistic data size, not at the dependency-landing step itself.

The double-recurrence of this affirm slug co-occurring with the pinned graduation recommendation is a joint signal: don't just add a memory note — upgrade the exercise-based-verification rule itself to formally embed the reliability-over-performance motivation as the why, and the dependency-landing window as the canonical example of 'first realistic opportunity.' A rule with a motivation resists erosion under time pressure in a way a bare trigger rule does not.

from_slug: J — Goal-over-instruction: treat user input as hypothesis
instruction: J (treat user input as hypothesis) and reliability-over-performance are the same epistemic stance at different scopes: J applies it at the utterance level, pinned applies it at the execution level. Chain them: when J fires, the full chain is instruction → goal → evidence-from-exercise, not instruction → assumed-goal → done. The reliability axis closes J's loop — you can't confirm the goal is met without exercising it against real data.

from_slug: J — Goal-over-instruction: treat user input as hypothesis
instruction: When writing the global memory entry for reliability-over-performance, add J as the upstream step the pinned note omits: the full hierarchy is goal-identification (J) → structured planning → exercise-at-first-opportunity → reliability evidence. The three-way unification the pinned note proposes (exercise-based-verification + structure-over-one-shotting + efficacy-over-speed) is incomplete without J as the entry point — you can't structure-over-one-shot toward the right outcome unless you first identify the goal, not just the instruction.

Annotate the reliability-over-performance global memory entry with its primary bug class: scale-sensitive bugs (ARG_MAX class — input-size assumptions only violated at real data, not toy inputs). This calibrates when the 'exercise at first opportunity' trigger is highest-urgency: any feature that processes user data of unknown or variable size. The ARG_MAX incident is the canonical example; naming the class makes the rule predictive, not just retrospective.

The S3 vocabulary-mismatch event shows a perceived structural difference was accepted as abstraction justification without falsification. Pin-20260624205747-54's design→adversarial-skeptic→empirical-test loop is the missing corrective: the skeptic's explicit job is to falsify the justification claim before any code is written. Add to the vocabulary-mismatch precheck: 'Can the state-count gap be handled with a mapping expression at the render callsite?' — if yes, the justification fails falsification and the abstraction is blocked.

Three S3 fires despite a live rule confirms pin-20260624080929-6d's finding that slugs are diagnostic but not corrective mid-session. The precheck ('does the future caller exist NOW?') fires in the coding turn — too late. The instruction from the pinned success case: move this gate to the planning turn. Before any design session that proposes a new layer, the precheck must appear in the task plan as a blocking question, not as an in-session hint that fires after coding has already begun.

Pin-20260624205747-54 shows atone slugs used as a design-time predictive checklist successfully killed an over-build before code was written. The S3 vocabulary-mismatch slug does not yet appear in that checklist — it was named after the session where it fired. Add vocabulary-mismatch-as-abstraction-license to the standard design-phase checklist alongside speculative-abstractions, with its empirical falsification test ('can a callsite adapter eliminate the vocabulary gap?') as the required answer before the design proceeds.

Pin-20260624080929-6d's double-recording slip — CLI background silence misread as process death — shares the cognitive fingerprint of the speculative-abstractions trigger: an absent signal (no observed CLI output; no existing caller) is interpreted as confirmation of a pre-formed hypothesis. Extend the speculative-abstractions precheck to surface this pattern explicitly: 'Am I treating the absence of a counter-argument as evidence for my hypothesis, rather than requiring positive confirmation that a caller exists?'

Pin-20260624205747-54 shows that an existing rule's slug used as a design-time checklist item prevented recurrence. The S3 vocabulary-mismatch slug is a variant entry point into the same failure class as speculative-abstractions. Rather than graduating it as a peer rule (splitting the checklist surface), embed it as a named trigger path inside the speculative-abstractions rule: 'one known entry point is a vocabulary or state-count mismatch accepted as structural justification without a callsite-adapter falsification test.' Single rule, multiple named entry points — easier to maintain and harder to miss.

The S3+S2 same-slug recurrence in atone (user semantics not restated before coding the default branch) maps directly onto the 60+-day-rotted hygiene proposals: both share the pattern of treating prior stated intent as assumed rather than verified. Before each proposal is implemented, the agent must re-read the original proposal rationale and restate the user's literal intent in the implementation plan — not infer it from the proposal title alone.

The S2 'deliberate-user-pattern-silently-removed-during-refactor' slug directly threatens the atone-system fragility proposal cluster: a refactor pass on the fragile atone system could silently drop load-bearing behaviors that were deliberate. Before any atone-system proposal is implemented, enumerate what the current code does deliberately (esp. kernel-append-only flag, slug recurrence tracking) and verify each is preserved post-change.

The S2 'scoped-instruction-carried-into-durable-artifact' failure matches the proposals pattern where two proposals describe the same atone --rca bug from opposite angles: a scoped per-session fix written into one proposal may have leaked into the framing of the other. Merge the two conflicting proposals by first identifying which framing reflects the durable system invariant versus which reflects a session-scoped observation, then discard the session-scoped one.

The repeated 'inverted-user-defined-opt-in-polarity' slug (appearing twice, indicating recurrence) correlates with the cli-gating hook redesign proposal that has been tune-flagged twice by consecutive dream passes: an opt-in/opt-out polarity inversion is exactly the class of error the cli-gating redesign is meant to prevent. Treat the redesign proposal as high-urgency and explicitly encode the correct polarity (opt-in = disabled by default, user enables) in the spec before implementation.

The June 26 ledger cluster in proposals is new and unscheduled — the same recency that makes it feel low-risk makes it the highest risk for the 'user-defined-semantics-not-restated' failure mode, since the user's intent is freshest and most likely to be assumed rather than re-read. Before scheduling the ledger build-gate step, explicitly restate the ledger cluster's acceptance criteria in the implementation task description.
